label: fiction
title: The Miller's Measure
charge: Theft by false measure — taking excess toll from the manor's grain
elements: The toll-dish used at the mill was false.
The miller knew it, and used it to take excess grain.

beats.text: The reeve himself concedes it before the jurors: in a lean year, every miller on the river is called a thief. It is the oldest slander in the village, and this year the village is hungry.
beats.reveal_note: True, and honestly said: the proverb has wronged innocent millers for a hundred years. It also taught this one exactly what he could hide behind.

beats.text: The toll-dish is set against the king's standard measure before the whole court: a tenth heavy. Warmed at the brazier, it yields up a false bottom of blackened wax.
beats.reveal_note: The dish speaks for itself: a tenth of every man's grain, taken by a bottom that melts. The village's grudge needed no help this year.

beats.text: The carter who hauls for three farms: the miller alone handled the toll-dish, and kept it locked in the mill chest between market days.
beats.reveal_note: The wax needed a keeper. One key, one hand, one beneficiary.

beats.text: The steward's charge to the jurors: prejudice against a trade convicts no one — but neither does it acquit. Weigh the dish, not the proverb.
beats.reveal_note: Both halves matter: the village's grudge proved nothing, and excused nothing. The wax was real.

twist: Everyone hates the miller — which is exactly why 'everyone knows' proves nothing, and exactly what he was counting on. The dish settled it without the village's help: a tenth heavy, with a wax false bottom that melted by the courtroom brazier.
